perturbation.original_text: 2. Definition. "Confidential Information" means any information, technical data, or know-how, including, but not limited to, that which relate s to research, product plans, products, services, customers, markets, software, developments , inventions, processes, designs, drawings, engineering, hardware configuration informati on, marketing or finances, which Confidential Information is designated in writing to be confid ential or proprietary, or if given orally, is confirmed promptly in writing as having been disclosed as confidential or proprietary.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. Definition. "Confidential Information" means any information, technical data, or know-how, including, but not limited to, that which relates to research, product plans, products, services, customers, markets, software, developments, inventions, processes, designs, drawings, engineering, hardware configuration information, marketing or finances, which Confidential Information is designated in writing to be confidential or proprietary. Confidential Information disclosed orally need not be confirmed in writing.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement for oral disclosures of confidential information to be confirmed in writing, this change introduces ambiguity. Previously, all confidential information had to be designated in writing or confirmed promptly in writing if disclosed orally. Now, oral disclosures are considered confidential without written confirmation. This creates an in-text contradiction if, for instance, section 3 refers back to this definition assuming that all 'Confidential Information' has been documented. Now that this section does not require to be confirmed in writing, the contradiction arises in Section 3.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3. Non-Disclosure of Confidential Information. The Recipient agrees not to use the Confidential Information for any purpose other than that set forth in Section 1 of this Agreement. The Recipient will not disclose any Confidential Inform ation to third parties except those directors, officers, employees, consultants and agents of R ecipient who are required to have the information in order to carry out the purpose set forth in Section 1 of this Agreement. Recipient has had or will have directors, officers, employees, cons ultants and agents of Recipient to whom Confidential Information is disclosed or who have access to Confidential Information sign a Non- Disclosure Agreement in content substantially simi lar to this Agreement and will promptly notify the Discloser in writing of the names of each su ch person who has signed such agreements after such agreements are signed. Recipient agrees th at it will take all reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure or use of Confidential Information in order to prevent it from falling into the public domain or the possession of persons other than those persons authorized hereunder to have any such information, which measures shall include the highest degree of care that Recipient utilizes to protect its own Confiden tial Information of a similar nature. Recipient agrees to notify the Discloser in writing of any misuse or misappropriation of such Confidential Information which may come to its attention.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement for oral disclosures of confidential information to be confirmed in writing. The original text requires that 'Confidential Information' if disclosed orally, is confirmed promptly in writing. The changed text states that 'Confidential Information disclosed orally need not be confirmed in writing'. This contradicts Section 3, 'Non-Disclosure of Confidential Information', which assumes that all Confidential Information is documented, especially concerning the notification of individuals who have signed Non-Disclosure Agreements after accessing Confidential Information. Without written confirmation of oral disclosures, the recipient cannot be certain of who has access to 'Confidential Information' and if they signed a NDA, and therefore breaches Section 3. This creates a strong in-text contradiction, per the definition provided for 'Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Return of Materials. Any materials or doc uments of which have been furnished by the Discloser to the Recipient will be promptly re turned within five (5) business days, accompanied by copies of such documentation, after the evaluatio n set forth in Section 1 of this Agreement has been concluded.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Return of Materials. Any materials or documents of which have been furnished by the Discloser to the Recipient *may* be returned when the Discloser deems it necessary. The time frame for the return will be dependent on the Discloser needs.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will be promptly returned within five (5) business days' to '*may* be returned when the Discloser deems it necessary. The time frame for the return will be dependent on the Discloser needs' creates a significant ambiguity regarding the return of materials. The original text imposed a clear obligation on the Recipient with a specific timeframe. The modified text makes the return discretionary and time-dependent on Discloser needs, negating the previous obligation. This contradicts the implicit assumption in Section 6 that the Recipient will have returned all materials after the evaluation period, introducing uncertainty about whether the Recipient still has the Confidential Information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6. No License Granted. Nothing in this Agreement is intended to grant any rights to Recipient under any patent, copyright, trade secret or other intellectual property right nor shall this Agreement grant Recipient any rights in or to the other party's Confidential Information, except the limited right to review such Confidentia l Information solely for the purpose set forth in Section 1 of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text in Section 5 mandated the return of materials within five business days. The changed text makes the return discretionary ('may be returned when the Discloser deems it necessary') and dependent on the Discloser's needs, removing the fixed timeframe. This directly contradicts the implicit assumption in Section 6, which grants the Recipient a 'limited right to review such Confidential Information solely for the purpose set forth in Section 1'. If the materials are not promptly returned as initially stated, it undermines the 'limited right' defined in Section 6, creating uncertainty and a direct contradiction concerning the Recipient's possession and permitted use of the Confidential Information, therefore leading to a strong contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 8. Term. The foregoing commitments shall survive any termination of discussions between the parties, and shall continue for a peri od of five (5) years following the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. Term. The foregoing commitments shall survive any termination of discussions between the parties, and shall continue *unless otherwise notified by the Discloser*.
perturbation.explanation: By adding 'unless otherwise notified by the Discloser,' the commitment's term is no longer fixed at five years. The Discloser can unilaterally terminate the confidentiality obligations at any time. This creates an in-text contradiction because Section 9 assumes the confidentiality obligations will last a specific duration (five years), potentially affecting the interpretation of successors' and assigns' obligations related to the information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 9. Miscellaneous. This Agreement shall be binding upon and for the benefit of the undersigned parties, their successors and assigns, provided that Confidential Information may not be assigned without the prior written consent of th e Discloser. Failure to enforce any provision of this Agreement shall not constitute a waiver of any term hereof.
perturbation.justification: The original text in Section 8 specifies a fixed five-year term for the commitments. The changed text in Section 8 introduces the possibility of termination by the Discloser at any time ('unless otherwise notified by the Discloser'). This directly contradicts Section 9, which assumes a binding agreement on successors and assigns, implying a specific duration for the confidentiality obligations. The modification introduces ambiguity about the duration, creating an in-text contradiction because Section 9's provisions are now subject to the Discloser's unilateral termination right, undermining the binding nature of the agreement on successors and assigns, as the term is no longer fixed.

perturbation.original_text: 13. Governing Law and Jurisdiction. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with the inte rnal laws of the State of New York (without regard to its conflict of law rules), and shall be binding upon the parties hereto in The United States of America and worldwide. The state cour ts within Monroe County New York and/or the federal courts located in Western New York shall have exclusive jurisdiction to adjudicate any dispute arising out of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 13. Governing Law and Jurisdiction. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with the laws of the Recipient's state of incorporation. The parties agree to non-exclusive jurisdiction for any dispute arising out of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This modification replaces the explicit choice of New York law and exclusive jurisdiction in Monroe County/Western New York federal courts with the laws of the Recipient's state and non-exclusive jurisdiction. This directly contradicts the original agreement. The change creates an in-text contradiction with Section 10 (Severability) because the severability clause is predicated on New York law being applied to the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 10. Severability. If any part of this Agreement should be held inva lid by operation of law or by a tribunal of competent jurisdiction, the balance of this Agreement shall continue in full force and effect. The part held invalid shall be modified as required by law or the tribunal of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies New York law and exclusive jurisdiction in New York courts. The changed text replaces this with the law of the Recipient's state and non-exclusive jurisdiction. This directly contradicts the original agreement. As the explanation states, this change creates an in-text contradiction with Section 10 (Severability) because the severability clause is predicated on New York law being applied to the agreement. The change in governing law directly impacts the severability clause, creating a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 14. Remedies. Recipient agrees that its obligations hereunder are necessary and reasonable in order to protect the Discloser and its business, and expressly agrees that monetary damages would be inadequate to compensate the Discloser for any breach of any covenants and agreements set forth herein. Accordingly, R ecipient agrees and acknowledges that any such violation or threatened violation will cause irreparable injury to the Discloser and that, in addition to any other remedies that may be available, in la w, in equity or otherwise, the Discloser shall be entitled to obtain injunctive relief against the threatened breach of this Agreement or the continuation of any such breach, without the necessity of proving actual damages.
perturbation.changed_text: 14. Remedies. Recipient agrees that monetary damages will be the primary remedy for any breach of this agreement. The Discloser waives the right to seek injunctive relief.
perturbation.explanation: This change directly contradicts the original agreement, which stated monetary damages would be inadequate and the Discloser was entitled to injunctive relief. This change limits the Discloser's remedies to monetary damages and removes the possibility of injunctive relief. This change creates a contradiction with the agreement's initial claim that protecting the Discloser and its business is necessary and reasonable because the remedies are weakened to standard damages.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 15. Entire Agreement. This Agreement, em bodies the entire agreement and understanding by and between the parties with respect to th e subject matter herein referred to, and no representations, promises, agreements, or understandings, written or oral, not herein contained shall be of any force or effect. No change or modification shall be valid or binding unless the same is in writing and signed by both parties. IN WITNESS WHEREOF, the parties hereto ha ve executed this Agreement as of the date first written above. ROCHESTER INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY Signed:_________________________ Name:_________________________ Title:_____________ _____________ Date: Signed:_________________________________ Name:_________________________________ Title:_______________ ___________________ Date:
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the contradicted text. The original text in section 14 stated that monetary damages would be inadequate and the Discloser was entitled to injunctive relief. The changed text limits the Discloser's remedies to monetary damages and removes the possibility of injunctive relief. Section 15, Entire Agreement, states that no change or modification shall be valid or binding unless the same is in writing and signed by both parties, which creates a contradiction. Thus, the modification in Section 14 causes a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 7. Export Control. Discloser has categori zed this project as export controlled under the International Traffic in Arms Regulation (ITAR ) or the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) and the applicable Export Control Regulations of the United States. As a result, certain technical data, hardware, software or other information furnished to Recipient shall be labeled “Export Controlled.” Other specific technical information created by the Recipient may be export controlled as well if it relates to the specific produc t design or prototype to be created in this project. Recipient agrees that export controlled information shall not be disclosed to any unauthorized foreign person, firm, country, incl uding foreign persons employed by Discloser. Recipient understands there are civil and criminal penalties for failure to comply with U.S. Export Control laws and will comply with all applicable laws while performing services for Discloser. Specifically, Recipient agrees to comply with U.S. export control laws and regulations on all information it receives that is labeled “Export Controlled” and on information it creates in the scope of work on this project that is export c ontrolled. Recipient further warrants that all individuals that will be given access to this info rmation are U.S. citizens or permanent U.S. residents with a valid permanent resident cards, and that the information disclosed to Recipient will be stored securely. If Recipient has any qu estions concerning this clause, Recipient should contact Discloser immediately.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. Export Control. The parties acknowledge that the project *may* involve export-controlled information. However, the Recipient is responsible for determining whether any information it receives or creates is subject to export control regulations. The Discloser will not label any information as "Export Controlled." The Recipient is responsible for compliance if deemed applicable.
perturbation.explanation: This modification introduces a significant contradiction regarding export control. The original text states that the Discloser categorizes the project as export-controlled and labels relevant information. The Recipient agreed to comply with these controls. The modified text shifts the responsibility to the Recipient to determine if export controls apply, with the Discloser explicitly *not* labeling information. This now creates an in-text contradiction to Section 3, since it discuss of confidential information to those people required to have it, and this includes directors, officers, employees, consultants and agents of Recipient who are required to have the information in order to carry out the purpose set forth in Section 1 of this Agreement, and now with the changing in Section 7, those that are required, may not be the correct people, due to the the new responsabilities.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3. Non-Disclosure of Confidential Information. The Recipient agrees not to use the Confidential Information for any purpose other than that set forth in Section 1 of this Agreement. The Recipient will not disclose any Confidential Inform ation to third parties except those directors, officers, employees, consultants and agents of R ecipient who are required to have the information in order to carry out the purpose set forth in Section 1 of this Agreement. Recipient has had or will have directors, officers, employees, cons ultants and agents of Recipient to whom Confidential Information is disclosed or who have access to Confidential Information sign a Non- Disclosure Agreement in content substantially simi lar to this Agreement and will promptly notify the Discloser in writing of the names of each su ch person who has signed such agreements after such agreements are signed. Recipient agrees th at it will take all reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure or use of Confidential Information in order to prevent it from falling into the public domain or the possession of persons other than those persons authorized hereunder to have any such information, which measures shall include the highest degree of care that Recipient utilizes to protect its own Confiden tial Information of a similar nature. Recipient agrees to notify the Discloser in writing of any misuse or misappropriation of such Confidential Information which may come to its attention.
perturbation.justification: The original text in Section 7 placed responsibility on the Discloser for categorizing and labeling export-controlled information, with the Recipient agreeing to comply. The changed text in Section 7 shifts this responsibility to the Recipient and removes the Discloser's obligation to label information. This directly contradicts Section 3, which requires the Recipient to disclose confidential information only to those with a need to know within the scope of Section 1, under the premise that the Discloser has identified and labeled export-controlled information. Because this labeling is not longer happening, the information may be going to someone who is not authorized to have it, creating a clear and strong conflict.
